item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
The image that was used in this article has been replaced with the following image, which is cc-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0):

Source: Flickr
License: CC BY 2.0
Title: cleaner with mop and uniform cleaning hall floor of public business building
Author: Aqua Mechanical
Release year: 2016

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As posted by a few other users, one of the previous changes has made things a little confusing in this article. The new version of the article posted on November 21st, 2016 (by Roget) removed the line

If the head of SCP-1837-1 comes into contact with a solid object, the contacted area will be transmuted into a corrosive liquid, known as SCP-1837-2.

This removes references to both transmuting and that the acid is known as SCP-1837-2. This leaves an undefined -2 later in the paragraph and makes the addendum make less sense as the transmutation that happens is never mentioned before (though that could just be a surprise effect that happened during that incident).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
obtaining various cleaning materials to try and remove SCP-1837-2

Is 1837-2 referring to the acid? This is the only time in the document that this designation is used. If it is, I would suggest a small editting to make it consistent through the rest of it. Other than that it's OK.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
During the re-containment operations, SCP-1837 was found surrounded by a large pool of SCP-1837.

Methinks this should be a large pool of 1957?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm a little confused - does the mop head produce acid, or does it turn the things it touches into acid? The first part of the description says one, but the addendum implies the other.

Also, if the mop head is the only part that makes the acid, why not suspend it in the liquid by the handle? That seems like it would be less risky than depending on currents which could stop if the power goes out.

Those nitpicks aside, I really like this one. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: E-class objects are anomalies which haven't been given scp designations yet.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What is E-████,? I know there have been other classes then "D." Is this one of those, or is it an item? What is E designation?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is great. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This skip made me get an account so I could upvote it. Not even kidding.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: ғᴏʀᴇɪɢɴ ᴄᴏɴᴛᴀᴍɪɴᴀɴᴛ

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: D'awww. This thing is precious and we shall call it 'Mr. Clean' and give it pinesol and a little nametag and together with 447 they will fight crime and expose magicians!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really like this. I can just imagine it accidentally creating acid and then thinking "Sorry, sorry, i made such a mess, I'll clean it up! Oh god, oh god, it keeps getting worse! I'm so sorry! I promise, I'll clean this right up! Just give me one… oh dear, it just keeps getting worse! I'm sorry! I'm sorry!" As it desperately tried to clean its mess and just makes more acid.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
FFFFFF——

This is pretty boring, to be honest. But the addendum is so charming.

Can't decide how to vote, so no vote for now.

EDIT: On second read, the addendum is worth an upvote all on its own.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yes. Yes you did. *patpat* :-P

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I came up with that line.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: As I said in chat, I totally imagine the mop chasing the invaders going "YOU'RE! MAKING! THINGS! DIIIIIRTYYYYY!!!!" *angry mopping*

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
But, wouldn't it be just creating more acid when it cleans rubble and whatnot?

Yes, exactly. Not only is it failing to clean things up, it's making things worse as it tries.

"I'M WHY WE CAN'T HAVE NICE THINGS!!!"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Someone's trapped in there. Or not. I'd like to think that someone is.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Aha, now I see it, thanks.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think the idea is that it uses means other than its mop end to clean stuff up.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This kind of confused me. So, when it touches something that thing turns into acid. And then, the SCP will attempt to clean up the acid or damage caused by it. But, wouldn't it be just creating more acid when it cleans rubble and whatnot? Or was that implied in the article and I just missed it?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The only thing I can think is, it turns whatever it touches into some kind of corrosive…

So, the entire floor then?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like the base concept, and I've always wanted to see an SCP created by cross-contamination of other SCPs, but … I don't really like this SCP for some reason. Maybe it's because the SuperAcid doesn't seem detailed and real-world-chemistry-ish enough, or maybe the thing about the trying-to-help-but-causing-more-damage is a little cliché-y … I don't know. No vote for the moment. Maybe I'll change my mind once I wake up.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The cleaning materials bits was really cute. I think that whole paragraph really pushed what you were going for.

Also, I loved the addendum. I feel like I shouldn't but…as Mr Wilt said, it's just so charming.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I could see it. Dunno if it'd make me like it less, though, but I definitely see where you're coming from.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Whoops. Got you two a bit confused.

I have no idea what this would be a god of, but do you see why I get that impression? The sort of earnest yet warped compulsion the thing has, the fact that it's very good at corroding and mildly decent at cleaning. Dunno, it's just a feel.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this; it's charming and quaint, and other than being a bit bare, it works.

That being said, I absolutely hate that I like this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What would this be a god of ?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Also, as an aside, it's not uncommon for people to hold off on tags. If one doesn't feel confident adding the tags, there's nothing wrong with waiting for someone with more experience to help out with them.

That's not the case here though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Good point, I changed it so that the samples were already diluted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Actually Voct is the one with the lobotogods.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This really reads another one of your lobotomized godlings.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Minor quibble: The corrosive material that eats through anything it touches is to be contained in the biohazard facility how? Does the acid not run out of 'eating power' the way real life acid does? Even if it remained potent forever it would continually eat through whatever it is being stored in.

The idea of a horribly corrosive mop that feels bad about destroying things and really, is just trying to help is charming. I upvoted hard.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Aww, so it saved the day in the end. This really is bittersweet.

I'm glad to see you did this. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
In some ways, it's almost as if the SCP is trying to contain itself.
I also like the idea of it being created due to contamination from other SCPs.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks to drewbear for helping me out with the whole thing, especially the addendum.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's been up for like 2 minutes, hold your horses son. I've added the tags now, after I announced it and renamed the list.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Add your tags please.

Nevermind.
